coredata.dc:title: Sensitive electrochemiluminescent detection of telomerase activity based on nicking enzyme assisted signal amplification
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Shu-Ying
Pan, Chang-Gang
Dai, Yu-Hua
Liang, Guo-Xi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, an ultrasensitive biosensor for the detection of telomerase activity was constructed by utilizing gold@Carbon dots nanohybrids (Au@CDs) as a high efficiency electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitter and nicking enzyme assisted signal amplification (NESA) technology. Firstly, the self-enhanced Au@CDs was synthesized from the reduction of HAuCl4 with carbon dots (CDs), which served as reductant and stabilizer. The prepared hybrids exhibited significant enhancement in the ECL emission compared to the single CDs. Enhanced sensitivity was thus achieved by using these Au@CDs as ECL labels. Next, an isothermal amplification with nicking enzyme (Nb.BbvcI)-assisted recycle was employed to convert a small amount of telomerase to numerous output signal DNA (sDNA). The obtained sDNA was then linked with Au@CDs and immobilized on a capture DNA (cDNA), and connected to the working electrode through oligonucleotide hybridization, resulting in a quantitative ECL reading. As a consequence, by employment of the dual signal amplification strategy, the proposed biosensor exhibited excellent sensitivity for telomerase activity detection with a detection limit down to 1 HeLa cell, which is comparable or even better than the most reported methods. The method also displayed a wide linear response ranges and good reproducibility. This work not only expanded the application of high-efficiency ECL indicators Au@CDs, but also offered a novel PCR-free ECL sensing platform for detecting telomerase activity that is helpful for real-time monitoring, point-of-care testing and on-site analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Telomerase
Nicking enzyme assisted signal amplification
Electrochemiluminescence
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of the telomerase activity detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053) and Advanced Talents Science Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 10JDG038, No. 10JDG052).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PAGE analysis of distinct samples: (A) lane 1, TP; lane 2, aDNA1; lane 3, TP/aDNA1; lane 4, TP/aDNA1 + telomerase + dNTPs. (B) lane 1, aDNA1; lane 2, aDNA2; lane 3, aDNA3; lane 4, aDNA1 + aDNA2 + aDNA3; lane 5, aDNA1 + aDNA2 + aDNA3 + Nb.BbvcI. The concentrations of DNA strands were all 2 μM.

(A) Nyquist plot of EIS data collected from bare or modified GE after proper modification: (a) bare GE, (b) cDNA/GE, (c) MCH/cDNA/GE, (d) sDNA/MCH/cDNA/GE, (e) Au@CDs-sDNA/MCH/cDNA/GE. Electrolyte: 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, biasing potential: 0.210 V. (B) ECL responses of the biosensor with or without telomerase.
Effects of (A) concentration of TP-aDNA1 duplex, (B) concentration of aDNA2, (C) concentration of aDNA3 and (D) amount of Nb.BbvcI on ECL response of the biosensor.

(A) ECL profiles of Au@CDs-based biosensor obtained with telomerase activity from 1.6 × 10-11 IU to 8 × 10-8 IU. (B) Plot of ECL peak intensity vs the logarithm of telomerase activity. (C) ECL profiles of Au@CDs-based biosensor obtained with HeLa cell from 5 to 3000. (D) Plot of ECL peak intensity vs the logarithm of HeLa cell number.

(A) Specificity of the ECL biosensor for telomerase. (B) Reproducibility of the ECL biosensor. (C) Stability of the ECL biosensor storing for 15 days. (D) Evaluation of telomerase activity from different cell lines and the heat-inactivated negative controls. The cell extracts were equivalent to 2000 cells. Error bars, SD, n = 3.
The schematic illustration of the fabrication procedure of the ECL biosensor based on NESA for ultrasensitive detection of telomerase activity.
Sequences of oligonucleotides used in this study.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Marked red is the cleaving sequence of Nb.BbvCI.

head.ce:title: Sensitive electrochemiluminescent detection of telomerase activity based on nicking enzyme assisted signal amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environment Science, School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environment Science, School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, an ultrasensitive biosensor for the detection of telomerase activity was constructed by utilizing gold@Carbon dots nanohybrids (Au@CDs) as a high efficiency electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitter and nicking enzyme assisted signal amplification (NESA) technology. Firstly, the self-enhanced Au@CDs was synthesized from the reduction of HAuCl4 with carbon dots (CDs), which served as reductant and stabilizer. The prepared hybrids exhibited significant enhancement in the ECL emission compared to the single CDs. Enhanced sensitivity was thus achieved by using these Au@CDs as ECL labels. Next, an isothermal amplification with nicking enzyme (Nb.BbvcI)-assisted recycle was employed to convert a small amount of telomerase to numerous output signal DNA (sDNA). The obtained sDNA was then linked with Au@CDs and immobilized on a capture DNA (cDNA), and connected to the working electrode through oligonucleotide hybridization, resulting in a quantitative ECL reading. As a consequence, by employment of the dual signal amplification strategy, the proposed biosensor exhibited excellent sensitivity for telomerase activity detection with a detection limit down to 1 HeLa cell, which is comparable or even better than the most reported methods. The method also displayed a wide linear response ranges and good reproducibility. This work not only expanded the application of high-efficiency ECL indicators Au@CDs, but also offered a novel PCR-free ECL sensing platform for detecting telomerase activity that is helpful for real-time monitoring, point-of-care testing and on-site analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Telomerase is a specialized ribonucleoprotein, which can realize the addition of repeating unit (TTAGGG)n onto the end of the human chromosome and maintain the length of telomere [1,2]. In normal somatic cells, the telomerase activity is inhibited, but it is overexpressed in the majority of cancer cells [3,4]. Thus, the reliable evaluation of telomerase activity is crucial for cancer diagnosis and therapy.

Now, the most commonly used methods for telomerase activity detection is the PCR-based telomeric repeat amplification protocol (TRAP), owing to its ultrahigh sensitivity and wide detection range [5,6]. However, it has the disadvantages of all PCR-based assays, including readily contaminated, time consuming, lacking simplicity, need thermal cycling instrument or skilled technician, imprecise quantitative information, and so on [7,8]. To avoid these issues, many PCR-free determination methods have been established, including fluorescence [9,10], chemiluminescence [11], colorimetric [12,13], electrochemical [14,15], and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [16] methods. However, due to the lack of an amplification mechanism, most do not achieve a sensitivity comparable to TRAP [17,18]. Therefore, the development of amplification protocols to improve the detection sensitivity of these methods is important.

Over the past decades, various isothermal nucleic acid amplification approaches, such as rolling circle amplification (RCA) [19], strand displacement amplification (SDA) [20], catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) [21], hybridization chain reaction (HCR) [22], and nicking enzyme assisted signal amplification (NESA) [23,24] have emerged as promising alternatives to PCR for signal amplification. Unlike PCR, these isothermal approaches do not require complex and expensive thermocyclers, thus can be performed at simple conditions (e.g., in a water bath). The simplicity and isothermal nature of these methods make them are promising for point-of-care testing and on-site analysis [25,26]. Moreover, since these methods are typically performed in a closed microsystem or portable device, the risk of contamination can be reduced [27]. NESA is a novel developed isothermal amplification technology that is performed in a one-step reaction with a group of help DNA and a nicking endonuclease. Compared with other isothermal amplification technique, the NESA offers superior advantages in terms of speed, specificity, cost efficiency and versatility, and has been successfully applied to a variety of molecules quantification, including nucleic acids, metal ions, proteins and some small molecules [28]. However, the sensitivity of NESA is still much lower than that of PCR [29]. Therefore, an additional amplification step is needed for ultra-low levels of targets detection.

Recently, with the development of nanotechnique, nanomaterial-based signal amplification opens new horizons for building highly sensitive assays and devices. Various nanomaterials, such as quantum dots (QDs), carbon nanomaterials, metal nanoparticles (NPs), magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and polymeric NPs with outstanding optical and electrical properties have been introduced to the analytical platforms as excellent signal reporters, transducers, and amplifiers for ultrasensitive detection [30–32]. Among such nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have aroused significant interest. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of luminescent carbon nanomaterials, which possess excellent luminescent properties, high biocompatibility and low toxicity, are widely used as signal reporters in fluorescence (FL), chemiluminescence (CL) and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) bioassays [33–35]. In addition, recently, our group found that when CDs served as reducing and stabilizing agents to reduce of Au ions forming gold@carbon dot nanohybrid (Au@CDs), they would be endowed with superior ECL emission [36]. Therefore, Au@CDs will be the ideal ECL luminophores for establishing highly sensitive biosensor. However, not really much ECL biosensing applications have been reported about Au@CDots till now.

In this work, utilizing the high luminous efficiency Au@CDs as efficient ECL indicators with the aid of NESA, a new PCR-free biosensor for telomerase activity detection was constructed (Scheme 1 ). The proposed biosensor realized the detection of telomerase activity from 5 to 3000 HeLa cells with a detection limit of 1 HeLa cell. Besides for the ultrahigh sensitivity and wide linear range, the biosensor also had good reproducibility, high selectivity, acceptable stability and simplicity, showing potential applications in clinical diagnostics and point-of-care testing.

3-[(3-Cholamidopropyl)-dimethylammonio]-1-propanesulfonate (CHAPS), glycerol, tris(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine (TCEP), 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO., USA). The nicking enzyme Nb.BbvCI and the corresponding buffer were bought from New England Biolabs, Inc. (NEB). All cancer cell lines, cell culture media and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from KeyGen Biotech. Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). All miRNA and DNA sequences can be seen in Table 1 . A telomerase detection kit containing telomerase standard solution (80 IU·L-1) was purchased from Innovation Beyond Limits (Germany). Phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride (PMSF), ethylene glycol tetraacetic acid (EGTA), deoxynucleotide solution mixture (dNTPs), and All miRNA and DNA sequences were purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Company, Ltd., and the sequences can be seen in Table 1. CaCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, FeCl3, Thrombin, acetylcholine (ACH) and Serotonin were brought from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were reagent grade. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (Milli-Q, 18 MΩ∙cm resistivity at 25 °C, Merck Millipore).

ECL measurements were performed using the MPI-E-ECL analyzer (Remax, China). Electrochemical-impedance-spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed using a Model CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc.). All ECL and electrochemical measurements were performed with a conventional three electrode system, using a modified gold electrode (GE, Φ = 3 mm) as working electrode, an Ag/AgCl as reference electrode and a platinum wire as counter electrode.

HeLa, HePG2, A549 and K562 cells were cultured in DMEM (Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium) containing 10% FBS and maintained in a 37 °C incubator with 5% CO2. MCF-7 cancer cells were cultured by the same procedure, but RPMI 1640 (Roswell Park Memorial Institute medium) was used as the medium.

The cells were collected by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 5 min), during the exponential growth, then rinsed twice with ice-cold sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 0.01 M, pH 7.4). Next, the cells were incubated in CHAPS lysis buffer (0.5% CHAPS, 10% glycerol, 1 mM MgCl2, 10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.5, 0.1 mM PMSF, 1 mM EGTA) for 30 min on ice. After centrifugation (12000 rpm, 4 °C, 20 min), the supernatant was collected and frozen at −80 °C before use. For the heat-inactivated experiments, cell extracts were heated at 95 °C for 20 min before detection.

TP and aDNA1 were dissolved in PBS and heated to 90 °C (5 min), then slowly cooled to room temperature to form a TP/aDNA1 duplex. Next, the TP/aDNA1 duplex (4 μL, 3 μM) was incubated with 20 μL of telomerase extract and 10 μL of telomerase extension reaction buffer (20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.3, 1.5 mM MgCl2, 63 mM KCl, 1 mM dNTPs, 1 mM EGTA, 0.005% Tween 20) at 37 °C for 1 h.

After the telomerization reaction, 4 μL of 3 μΜ aDNA2, 4 μL of 9 μΜ aDNA3 and 2.0 μL of Nb.BbvCI (80 U μL−1) were added to the preceding reaction system to perform the isothermal nicking enzyme reaction. After incubation at 37 °C for 2 h, the mixture was heated at 80 °C for 15 min to deactivate the Nb.BbvCI. The obtained solution contained massive NESA product, the signal DNA sequences (sDNA).

ECL luminophores Au@CDs nanoparticles were prepared according to our published works. The detailed ECL properties of Au@CDs also can be seen in our published works [36]. The above reaction product was mixed with TCEP (0.3 μL, 150 mM) for 30 min to active the thiol group of sDNA. Then, 50 μL of Au@CDs was added and incubate the mixture at room temperature for 1 h. The formed Au@CDs-sDNA was collected by centrifugation (12000 rpm, 5 min). After being washed with ultrapure water 3 times, the precipitate was dispersed in 25 μL of PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C for further use.

A gold electrode (GE) was scanned from 0 to + 1.5 V in 0.5 M H2SO4 until a remarkable voltammetric peak was obtained. Next, the electrode was incubated at 4 °C for 12 h with 10 µL of 2 µM cDNA to obtain cDNA modified electrode (cDNA/AuE) by Au-S bond. Furthermore, cDNA/AuE was immersed in 1 mM MCH for 1 h to seal the exposed parts of the electrode surface. Then the closed electrode (MCH/cDNA/GE) was thoroughly cleaned with ultrapure water and prepared for the detection of telomerase activity ECL.

Incubate the MCH/cDNA/GE biosensor electrode with the prepared Au@CDs-sDNA solution for 60 min to perform sDNA/cDNA hybridization, and then wash it with ultrapure water. The measurements of ECL were proceed with containing K2S2O8 (0.1 M) 5 mL of 0.1 M PBS, the measurement range for ECL was −1.5 to 0 V and PMT was set to 800 V.

A 12% native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) was used to analyze the products of telomerase reaction and isothermal amplification reaction. The gel electrophoresis was carried out in 1 × TBE buffer at constant voltage (150 V) for 40 min. After ethidium bromide (EB) staining, the gel was photographed with a digital camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the telomerase activity detection
ce:section.ce:para: Upon addition of telomerase, primer (TP) in TP-aDNA1 duplex could be extended to form more stable hairpin structures in the presence of deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates (dNTPs), leading to the release of aDNA1. The released aDNA1 then triggered the NESA by combining with aDNA2 and hairpin aDNA3 forming a “Y” junction structure that containing the recognition sequences for the nicking enzymes Nt.BbvCI. After nicking, the “Y” junction was destroyed, releasing a short-chain signal DNA (sDNA) and an aDNA1-aDNA2 duplex. The released aDNA1-aDNA2 duplex hybridized with another hairpin aDNA3 and initiated the next cycles, producing a large amount of sDNA. Subsequently, the ECL signal probe, Au@CDs-sDNA was fabricated by conjugated sDNA with Au@CDs. Au@CDs-sDNA was finally immobilized on capture DNA (cDNA)-modified sensing electrode surface via complementation to produce a quantitative ECL emission.

PAGE analysis was carried out to assess the feasibility of telomerase-induced primer elongation and the designed Nt.BbvCI-assisted NESA. As shown in Fig. 1 A, distinct single band for aDNA1, TP, and TP/aDNA1 in lanes 1, 2, and 3 were observed. From lane 4 (telomerase + dNTPs + TP/aDNA1), a band with much lower mobility were obtained, indicating the successful formation of hairpin TP structure, illustrating that the telomerase elongation process was carried out. Lanes 1, 2, and 3 in Fig. 1B showed the single bands for aDNA1, aDNA2, and aDNA3, respectively. After the aDNA1, aDNA2, and aDNA3 were hybridized, slower electrophoretic mobility can be studied in lane 4, confirming the formation of Y junction product. A new bright band appeared at the bottom of lane 5 (aDNA1 + aDNA2 + aDNA3 + Nb.BbvcI), which demonstrated that the cleavage process was carried out and massive shorter sequences of sDNA generated in the assistance of Nb.BbvcI.

Electrochemical-impedance-spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed using an electrochemical workstation. Fig. 2 A is the electrochemical impedance curve at each modification step. The diameter of the semicircle depends on the resistance to electron transfer. The exposed working electrode has small semicircular domains (curve a), indicating that electroactive ions are quickly transferred to the electrode surface. The subsequent self-assembly of cDNA, MCH, and sDNA, Ret increased gradually during the experiment (curve b-d), as the assembled dielectric prevented the redox probe from touching the electrode surface. Conjugation with Au@CDs decreased the Ret (curve e), suggesting that the Au@CDs accelerated the electron transfer due to their excellent conductivity. These results verified the successful fabrication of a telomerase activity biosensing interface.

Finally, ECL responses of the biosensor toward samples with and without telomerase were studied (Fig. 2B). As observed in curve a, the sample without telomerase only generated a background ECL signal. Whereas, in the presence of 3.2 × 10-8 IU telomerase, the ECL intensity significantly increased (curve b), demonstrating the telomerase activity sensing platform was successfully constructed.

The biosensor was based on the telomerase-triggered TP extension reaction, three strands (aDNA1, aDNA2, aDNA3) formed a “Y” junction structure that further cleaved by Nb.BbvcI for signal cycling. Thus, the choice of the concentrations of TP/aDNA1 duplex, aDNA2, and aDNA3 and the amount of Nb.BbvcI was critical in this study to achieve high sensitivity. The optimized results were shown in Fig. 3 , from which we can see that the maximum ECL signal was obtained under the optimized conditions of 3 µM TP/aDNA1 duplex, 3 µM aDNA2, 9 µM aDNA3 and 160 U Nb.BbvcI.

The performance of the biosensor was studied under optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, the constructed biosensor showed a good response to telomerase activity over a wide range of 1.6 × 10-11 −8 × 10-8 IU with a limit-of-detection (LOD, 3σ) of 5 × 10−12 IU. Moreover, the present biosensor can quantitatively determine the activity of telomerase in HeLa cell extracts with a linear range from 5 to 3000 cells and the LOD was as low as 1 cell (Fig. 4C and D). Compared with previous works, the proposed biosensor showed a comparable or more excellent detection limit, as shown in Table S1. Telomerase activity in a single HeLa cell was estimated to be 3.2 × 10-11 IU, in good agreement with previous reports [37,38]. To further reliably prove the activity of telomerase used in this work, a commercial human telomerase ELISA Kit was used to detect telomerase extracts from HeLa cell (Fig. S1). Comparative results between the standardized concentration from Elisa Kit and ECL value from our method exhibited the same tendencies, suggesting that this assay is reliable and general for telomerase activity determination.

In order to test the specificity of this ECL biosensor, several interferents including Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, thrombin, acetylcholine (ACH), and serotonin were tested to verify the anti-interference performance. As shown in Fig. 5 A, all these competitors did not interfere with the results of the proposed method. In addition, the reproducibility was also investigated by performing five parallel measurements toward same concentration of telomerase under the same experimental conditions (Fig. 5B), and low relative standard deviations (RSD) of 2.8% for intra-assay and 3.1% for inter-assay were observed, indicating a superior reproducibility of the proposed ECL biosensor. Next, the stability of this biosensor was also studies. As shown in Fig. 5C, a negligible change of the ECL intensity emerged, and the ECL signal remained 95% high with the RSD value of 3.7% after storing for 15 days, indicating that this telomerase ECL biosensor had good stability.

Finally, the practical utility of this ECL biosensor was studied by evaluating the telomerase activity in different cell lines. As shown in Fig. 5D, HeLa, A549 and K562 cells showed the highest telomerase activity compared to HepG2 and MCF-7 cells and compared to possible interference from real sample, while all heated cell sample displayed the negligible signal.

In summary, an ultrasensitive telomerase biosensor was established based on Au@CDs and telomerase-induced nicking enzyme assisted isothermal signal amplification (NESA). Owing to the high ECL efficiency of Au@CDs as well as the high amplification efficiency of NESA, the proposed method can sensitively measure telomerase with a detection limit as low as 1 HeLa cancer cell. Besides high sensitivity, it also possesses wide dynamic, good selectivity, satisfied reproducibility and acceptable stability. In addition, in comparison with the commonly used telomere repeat amplification protocol (TRAP), the proposed method does not require thermal cycling and can perform under an isothermal condition, is easy to be applied in point-of-care detection. We believe that the successful application of Au@CDs and the dual signal amplification strategy will inspire the development of novel sensitive ECL platform in many fields including clinical diagnostic, environmental monitoring and food analysis in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053) and Advanced Talents Science Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 10JDG038, No. 10JDG052).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106123.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The human telomere terminal transferase enzyme is a ribonucleoprotein that synthesizes TTAGGG repeats

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Morin, G. B, The human telomere terminal transferase enzyme is a ribonucleoprotein that synthesizes TTAGGG repeats, Cell. 59 (1989) 521-529.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Signal-on dual-potential electrochemiluminescence based on luminol-gold bifunctional nanoparticles for telomerase detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, M. Wu, J. Xu, H. Chen, Signal-on dual-potential electrochemiluminescence based on luminol-gold bifunctional nanoparticles for telomerase detection, Anal. Chem. 86 (2014) 3834-3840.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Specific association of human telomerase activity with immortal cells and cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.W. Kim, M.A. Piatyszek, K.R. Prowse, C.B. Harley, M.D. West, P.L. Ho, G.M. Coviello, W.E. Wright, S.L. Weinrich, J.W. Shay, Specific association of human telomerase activity with immortal cells and cancer, Science 266 (1994) 2011-2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A survey of telomerase activity in human cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.W. Shay, S.A. Bacchetti, A survey of telomerase activity in human cancer, Eur. J. Cancer 33 (1997) 787-791.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic bead and nanoparticle based electrochemiluminescence amplification assay for direct and sensitive measuring of telomerase activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhou, D. Xing, D. Zhu, L. Jia, Magnetic bead and nanoparticle based electrochemiluminescence amplification assay for direct and sensitive measuring of telomerase activity, Anal. Chem. 81 (2009) 255-261.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in quantification and characterization of telomerase activity by the telomeric repeat amplification protocol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. W. Kim, F. Wu,. Advances in quantification and characterization of telomerase activity by the telomeric repeat amplification protocol, Nucleic Acids Res. 25 (1997) 2595-2597.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assays for human telomerase activity: progress and prospects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhou, D. Xing, Assays for human telomerase activity: progress and prospects, Cheminform 41 (2012) 4643-4656.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reevaluation of telomerase inhibition by quadruplex ligands and their mechanisms of action

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.D. Cian, G. Cristofari, P. Reichenbach, E.D. Lemos, D. Monchaud, M. Teuladefichou, Reevaluation of telomerase inhibition by quadruplex ligands and their mechanisms of action, P. Natl. Acad. Sci. 104 (2007) 17347-17352.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence detection of DNA, adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP), and telomerase activity by zinc(II)-protoporphyrin IX/G-quadruplex labels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhang, E. Sharon, R. Freeman, X. Liu, I. Willner, Fluorescence detection of DNA, adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP), and telomerase activity by zinc(II)-protoporphyrin IX/G-quadruplex labels, Anal. Chem. 84 (2012) 4789-4797.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Real-time, quantitative lighting-up detection of telomerase in urines of bladder cancer patients by AIEgens

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Lou, Y. Zhuang, X. Zuo, Y.-M. Jia, Y. Hong, X. Min, Real-time, quantitative lighting-up detection of telomerase in urines of bladder cancer patients by AIEgens, Anal. Chem. 87 (2015) 6822-6827.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrogenerated chemiluminescence and its biorelated applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Miao, Electrogenerated chemiluminescence and its biorelated applications, Chem. Rev. 108 (2018) 2506-2553.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Catalytic beacons for the detection of DNA and telomerase activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xiao, V. Pavlov, T. Niazov, A. Dishon, M. Kotler, I. Willner, Catalytic beacons for the detection of DNA and telomerase activity, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 126 (2004) 7430-7431.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visualizing human telomerase activity with primer-modified Au aanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, L. Wu, J. Ren, X. Qu, Visualizing human telomerase activity with primer-modified Au aanoparticles, Small 8 (2012) 259-264.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive electrochemical detection of telomerase activity using spherical nucleic acids gold nanoparticles triggered mimic-hybridization chain reaction enzyme-free dual signal amplification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Wang, J. Li, K. Rui, P. Gai, J. Zhang, J. Zhu, Sensitive electrochemical detection of telomerase activity using spherical nucleic acids gold nanoparticles triggered mimic-hybridization chain reaction enzyme-free dual signal amplification, Anal. Chem. 87 (2015) 3019-3026.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free detection of telomerase activity in HeLa cells using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Yang, X. Zhu, Q. Liu, Z. Lin, B. Qiu, G. Chen, Label-free detection of telomerase activity in HeLa cells using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, Chem. Comm. 47 (2011) 3129-3131.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive telomerase activity detection by telomeric elongation controlled surface enhanced raman scattering

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zong, Z. Wang, H. Chen, Y. Cui, Ultrasensitive telomerase activity detection by telomeric elongation controlled surface enhanced raman scattering, Small, 9 (2013) 4215-4220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive telomerase activity detection in circulating tumor cells based on DNA metallization and sharp solid-state electrochemical techniques

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wu, J. Wang, J. Ren, X. Qu, Ultrasensitive telomerase activity detection in circulating tumor cells based on DNA metallization and sharp solid-state electrochemical techniques, Adv. Funct. Mater. 24 (2014) 2727-2733.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new approach to amplified telomerase detection with polyvalent oligonucleotide nanoparticle conjugates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Zheng, W.L. Daniel, C.A. Mirkin, A new approach to amplified telomerase detection with polyvalent oligonucleotide nanoparticle conjugates, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 130 (2008) 9644-9645.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mutation detection and single-molecule counting using isothermal rolling-circle amplification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.M. Lizardi, X. Huang, Z. Zhu, P. Brayward, D.C. Thomas, D.C. Ward, Mutation detection and single-molecule counting using isothermal rolling-circle amplification, Nat. Genet. 19 (1998) 225-232.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strand displacement amplification-an isothermal, in vitro DNA amplification technique
